family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis
Father: coronary artery disease (stent placed at age 58)
No family history of epilepsy or psychiatric illness

social_history.occupation: Software engineer at a technology company
social_history.alcohol: Rare, less than one drink per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a condominium; parents reside nearby
social_history.exercise: Regular yoga practice 3 times per week, occasional running

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but profoundly disoriented to time, place, and situation. Agitated and intermittently combative, requiring two staff members for safety. Speech is disorganized with frequent neologisms, perseveration, and tangential responses. Echolalia noted when questions are repeated. Unable to follow even one-step commands reliably. Short-term memory not testable. Glasgow Coma Scale fluctuates between 10 and 13.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Visual threat response present bilaterally. Facial sensation appears intact to grimace with noxious stimuli. Prominent perioral and orolingual dyskinesias with rhythmic tongue thrusting and lip pursing observed continuously. Gag reflex present. Tongue protrusion midline between episodes of involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.motor: Generalized increased tone in all four extremities with bilateral upper extremity rigidity greater than lower extremities. Strength grossly 4/5 in upper and lower extremities bilaterally when patient intermittently cooperates, but effort is unreliable. Intermittent choreiform movements of bilateral upper extremities and dystonic posturing of the right hand observed. No focal weakness identified.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws symmetrically to noxious stimuli in all four extremities. Formal sensory testing not possible due to encephalopathy and agitation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 3+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 3+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses equivocal bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to perform finger-to-nose or rapid alternating movements due to encephalopathy, agitation, and involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.gait: Not assessed; patient is bed-bound due to altered mental status and requires physical restraints intermittently for safety.
neurological_exam.additional: Pronounced orolingual dyskinesias with continuous rhythmic tongue thrusting, lip pursing, and chewing movements. Bilateral upper extremity choreiform and dystonic movements. Three episodes of sudden behavioral arrest with staring and oral automatisms lasting 10-20 seconds each observed during the 40-minute examination, highly suspicious for subclinical seizures. Autonomic instability evident with episodic tachycardia ranging from 105 to 145 bpm, diaphoresis, and blood pressure fluctuations between 130/82 and 168/98 mmHg. One brief episode of central hypoventilation observed with respiratory rate dropping to 8 breaths per minute for approximately 30 seconds before spontaneous recovery.

patient.chief_complaint: Worsening confusion, new-onset seizures, and involuntary movements over ten days
patient.history_present_illness: This 31-year-old left-handed woman of South Asian descent was brought to the emergency department by her parents after ten days of rapidly progressive neuropsychiatric decline. Approximately two and a half weeks prior to presentation, the patient developed a flu-like illness with myalgias, low-grade fever to 37.9 degrees Celsius, headache, and fatigue lasting five days. She recovered and returned to work, but within three days her mother noticed she was uncharacteristically irritable and tearful on the phone. Over the subsequent days the patient began expressing paranoid ideation, telling her parents that her coworkers were secretly monitoring her computer and conspiring to have her terminated. She stopped going to work, citing inability to concentrate and severe insomnia. Her manager contacted her parents after she failed to attend meetings for two consecutive days. When her parents visited her condominium six days before presentation, they found her apartment in disarray with uneaten food and found her sitting on the floor rocking and muttering incoherently. They brought her to an urgent care center where she was thought to have an acute psychotic episode and was referred to outpatient psychiatry. No medications were started. Over the following days at her parents' home, her confusion worsened dramatically. She developed repetitive tongue thrusting and lip pursing movements that occurred almost continuously. Four days before presentation, she had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately two minutes with post-ictal drowsiness lasting one hour. Her parents called emergency services and she was taken to a community hospital where she received IV lorazepam 2 mg and levetiracetam 1500 mg IV loading dose. A CT head without contrast was unremarkable and she was discharged the following day with an outpatient neurology referral and levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily. At home, she continued to deteriorate with worsening agitation, alternating with periods of near-unresponsiveness. She had a second generalized seizure on the day of presentation. Her parents noted that she had episodes of profuse sweating and that her heart seemed to be racing. Emergency medical services found her tachycardic at 125 bpm with blood pressure of 162/100 mmHg and she was transported to this tertiary care emergency department. She was unable to provide any history. Her parents confirmed no prior psychiatric history, no recent travel, no known tick exposure, and no new medications or supplements.

findings.location: Generalized, most prominent over central and frontal regions
findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta with superimposed 18-25 Hz beta activity
findings.morphology: High-amplitude rhythmic delta waves with superimposed low-amplitude beta bursts creating a characteristic brush-like pattern
findings.state: Present throughout wakefulness and drowsiness, persisting into light sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Extreme delta brush is a hallmark pattern highly specific to anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis

findings.location: Right temporal onset with rapid bilateral spread
findings.frequency: Three discrete electrographic seizures captured during the 45-minute recording, lasting 40-90 seconds each
findings.morphology: Rhythmic sharp-wave discharges at 3-4 Hz evolving into repetitive polyspikes
findings.state: Two occurred during apparent behavioral arrest with oral automatisms; one was clinically silent
findings.clinical_correlation: Frequent subclinical seizures indicate significant seizure burden requiring urgent treatment and continuous EEG monitoring

findings.morphology: High amplitude (150-250 microvolts), semi-rhythmic and quasi-periodic
findings.clinical_correlation: Severe diffuse cortical dysfunction consistent with encephalitis

artifacts.description: Frequent movement and muscle artifact from orolingual dyskinesias and upper extremity choreiform movements, partially obscuring frontotemporal channels

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed; patient unable to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response elicited; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Markedly abnormal EEG demonstrating extreme delta brush pattern, three electrographic seizures of right temporal origin with secondary generalization, and severe generalized rhythmic delta activity. The extreme delta brush pattern is considered highly specific for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The electrographic seizure burden is significant and warrants continuous EEG monitoring and urgent anti-seizure medication optimization.
eeg.limitations: Standard 45-minute recording; continuous EEG monitoring strongly recommended to characterize full seizure burden and detect nonconvulsive status epilepticus
recommended_actions: Urgent serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing
Consider ICU admission for close neurological monitoring

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes involving hippocampi and amygdalae, right slightly greater than left
findings.size: Right: 2.4 x 1.6 cm, Left: 1.8 x 1.2 cm

signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion (no corresponding ADC abnormality)

findings.borders: Ill-defined, merging with adjacent gray matter

additional_observations: Ventricular system normal in size and morphology
Normal major intracranial vessels on MRA time-of-flight sequences
mri.impression: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities involving the hippocampi and amygdalae without restricted diffusion, hemorrhage, or pathological enhancement, right greater than left. Subtle bilateral cingulate gyrus signal abnormality. These findings are consistent with autoimmune encephalitis or, less likely, viral encephalitis. The lack of hemorrhagic changes, restricted diffusion, and unilateral predominance argues against HSV encephalitis as the primary etiology.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDA receptor or other antibody-mediated)
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Bilateral symmetric medial temporal involvement without hemorrhage or restricted diffusion is the classic imaging pattern
Typically causes asymmetric temporal lobe changes with hemorrhage, edema, and restricted diffusion
Imaging appearance may be identical to antibody-mediated autoimmune encephalitis; clinical and serological correlation needed

recommended_actions: CSF analysis with autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel
Consider repeat MRI in 7-14 days to assess progression
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if autoimmune encephalitis confirmed to evaluate for underlying neoplasm

findings: Nonspecific T-wave flattening in leads V4-V6
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 118 bpm with nonspecific T-wave flattening in lateral precordial leads. QTc borderline prolonged at 448 ms. No acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Tachycardia consistent with autonomic instability in the setting of encephalitis. Nonspecific T-wave changes may be stress-related. QTc should be monitored with any QT-prolonging medications.

BMP.clinical_significance: Hyponatremia, likely SIADH secondary to CNS inflammation
Low-normal, consistent with dilutional effect of SIADH
Mildly elevated, may be stress-related or related to PCOS/insulin resistance

Inflammatory markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, nonspecific systemic inflammatory response

Other.clinical_significance: Elevated, likely from seizure activity, rigidity, and involuntary movements
Mildly elevated, can be seen post-seizure and in the setting of hypothalamic-pituitary axis dysfunction from encephalitis
Prediabetic range, consistent with PCOS-associated insulin resistance

labs.interpretation: Notable for hyponatremia (129 mEq/L) suggestive of SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, elevated CK from seizure activity and movement disorder, and mildly elevated prolactin likely post-ictal. Normal thyroid function argues against Hashimoto encephalopathy. Negative urine drug screen excludes drug-induced psychosis. Mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c consistent with known PCOS and insulin resistance. Normal hepatic and renal function.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 unique CSF bands, not matched in serum)
special_tests.Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes with activated morphology, no malignant cells identified

csf.interpretation: Significant lymphocytic pleocytosis (78 cells/uL, 93% lymphocytes) with mildly elevated protein and normal glucose ratio. Oligoclonal bands present with markedly elevated IgG index indicating robust intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis. All infectious PCRs and cultures are negative, effectively excluding common viral and bacterial meningoencephalitis. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive at a titer of 1:256, establishing the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Confirms systemic anti-NMDA receptor antibody production
CSF titer exceeds serum titer, confirming intrathecal synthesis and supporting anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis diagnosis

output.interpretation: Both serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG antibodies are positive. The CSF titer (1:256) is higher than the serum titer (1:128), demonstrating intrathecal antibody production. This pattern is the hallmark of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and confirms the diagnosis. CSF testing is more sensitive than serum; up to 15% of patients may be serum-negative but CSF-positive.

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 3.8 x 2.6 x 2.9 cm, contains multiple peripheral follicles consistent with polycystic morphology. No discrete mass identified.
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Polycystic morphology consistent with known PCOS, no teratoma

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 4.1 x 2.8 x 3.2 cm, contains a 2.3 cm complex mass with mixed echogenicity including echogenic foci suggestive of fat and calcification, consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). Mild internal vascularity on color Doppler.
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Left ovarian teratoma identified; ovarian teratomas are present in 40-50% of women with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and serve as the immunologic trigger

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal size 7.2 x 4.1 x 3.8 cm, homogeneous myometrium, endometrial stripe 8 mm
Small amount of free fluid in the posterior cul-de-sac, likely physiologic

output.interpretation: A 2.3 cm complex mass in the left ovary with mixed echogenicity, fat, and calcification consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). The right ovary shows polycystic morphology consistent with the patient's known PCOS. The left ovarian teratoma is the likely immunologic trigger for the patient's anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Surgical resection is strongly recommended, as tumor removal significantly improves neurological outcomes and reduces the risk of relapse.
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovary: 2.3 cm complex mass consistent with mature cystic teratoma

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.clinical_significance: Low-positive ANA, likely nonspecific given family history of autoimmune disease; does not indicate active lupus in the absence of other features

output.interpretation: The comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis panel confirms isolated anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity. All other neuronal surface antibodies (LGI1, CASPR2, GABA-B, AMPA, DPPX) are negative, excluding overlap syndromes. Anti-TPO is normal, excluding Hashimoto encephalopathy. ANA is low-positive at 1:80 with speckled pattern, which is nonspecific and present in up to 15% of the general population; it does not suggest CNS lupus in this clinical context. The results confirm isolated anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

signal_characteristics.density: Mixed density with fat attenuation (-30 to -50 HU), soft tissue, and punctate calcifications

additional_observations: No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation
Right ovary appears enlarged with multiple follicles consistent with polycystic morphology
output.impression: A 2.5 cm well-circumscribed left ovarian mass containing fat, soft tissue, and calcification, consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). No evidence of malignant features, regional lymphadenopathy, or distant metastatic disease. The teratoma is the presumed immunologic trigger for the patient's anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The right ovary shows polycystic morphology without discrete mass. No other primary neoplasm identified.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Fat attenuation, calcification, and well-circumscribed morphology are characteristic
Small size, well-circumscribed margins, and predominantly mature components argue against this

recommended_actions: Gynecologic oncology consultation for surgical planning
Oophorectomy or cystectomy of left ovarian teratoma

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands, unchanged from initial LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture shows persistent but mildly improving lymphocytic pleocytosis (62 cells/uL, down from 78) with slightly decreased protein compared to initial LP. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody titer remains at 1:256. The modest improvement in cell count and protein is encouraging but antibody titer reduction typically lags behind clinical and cytological improvement. Continued immunotherapy is warranted. Anticipate more significant improvement with combined immunotherapy and tumor resection.

Osmolality Studies.clinical_significance: Inappropriately concentrated relative to low serum osmolality, confirming SIADH
Elevated in the setting of hyponatremia, diagnostic of SIADH

output.interpretation: Hypotonic hyponatremia (serum osmolality 264 mOsm/kg) with inappropriately concentrated urine (548 mOsm/kg) and elevated urine sodium (52 mEq/L) establishes the diagnosis of SIADH. This is a well-recognized complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis due to hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction and CNS inflammation. Management includes fluid restriction to 1-1.2 L/day with close monitoring. Avoid rapid correction (target <10 mEq/L increase in 24 hours) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.
abnormal_values_summary: Urine sodium 52 mEq/L (elevated in setting of hyponatremia)

Pre-Immunotherapy Screening.clinical_significance: No latent TB; safe to proceed with immunosuppression
Baseline IgG normal; monitor for hypogammaglobulinemia if rituximab is used
Normal; IgA deficiency excluded (relevant for IVIG selection)

output.interpretation: Pre-immunotherapy screening is reassuring. All viral hepatitis, HIV, and tuberculosis screening tests are negative. Baseline immunoglobulin levels are normal, with no IgA deficiency (which would be a relative contraindication to certain IVIG preparations). The patient is cleared to proceed with first-line immunotherapy consisting of IV methylprednisolone and IVIG. If escalation to rituximab is needed, repeat hepatitis B screening and monitor immunoglobulin levels serially.

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis typically presents with acute high fevers, unilateral temporal lobe hemorrhagic changes on MRI, and positive CSF HSV PCR. This patient has bilateral non-hemorrhagic temporal FLAIR changes, negative HSV PCR, and a subacute rather than hyperacute course.
Isolated psychiatric illness would not explain seizures, orolingual dyskinesias, autonomic instability, CSF pleocytosis, or extreme delta brush on EEG. This patient has all of these features.

Requires antipsychotic exposure (absent here), presents with lead-pipe rigidity and markedly elevated CK (typically >1000 U/L). This patient has no antipsychotic exposure and CK is only moderately elevated at 245 U/L.
Characterized by markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies and often thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO antibodies, effectively excluding this diagnosis.
Presents with multifocal ischemic lesions on MRI and vessel irregularities on angiography. MRI in this case shows no ischemic changes, only inflammatory signal abnormality.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: ensure airway protection, establish IV access, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, administer IV lorazepam 2 mg for any active seizures
Obtain comprehensive initial labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, CK, hepatic panel, thyroid function, prolactin, urine drug screen, blood cultures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia (SIADH), elevated CK and inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities without hemorrhage or restricted diffusion

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis: cell count with differential, protein, glucose, Gram stain, bacterial culture, viral PCRs (HSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus), oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, oligoclonal bands with elevated IgG index, positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours while awaiting HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage for HSV encephalitis until excluded by PCR
Extreme delta brush pattern, electrographic seizures, generalized rhythmic delta activity

optimal_actions.action: Optimize anti-seizure medication: levetiracetam 1500 mg IV loading then 1000 mg IV every 12 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in electrographic and clinical seizure burden

optimal_actions.action: Once anti-NMDAR antibodies confirmed: initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days concurrently
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction of antibody-mediated neuronal damage and initiation of immunomodulation

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left ovarian teratoma identified, necessitating surgical resection

optimal_actions.action: Consult gynecologic oncology for urgent teratoma resection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical removal of immunologic trigger to improve outcomes and prevent relapse

optimal_actions.action: Implement fluid restriction (1-1.2 L/day) and serial sodium monitoring for SIADH management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Gradual sodium normalization, avoid correction >10 mEq/L per 24 hours

optimal_actions.action: If inadequate clinical response to first-line immunotherapy after 2-4 weeks: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 doses or cyclophosphamide
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical improvement with B-cell depletion or further immunosuppression

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric IV acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV corticosteroids and IVIG or plasma exchange) once diagnosis is confirmed
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic ultrasound or CT
Start anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Continuous EEG monitoring to detect and quantify subclinical seizures
Admit to ICU for monitoring of autonomic instability and airway protection
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder and discharging without workup for autoimmune encephalitis
Administering high-dose typical antipsychotics (e.g., haloperidol), which may worsen dyskinesias and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying immunotherapy while awaiting final antibody results when clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy when non-invasive testing has established the diagnosis
Rapidly correcting sodium (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome)
Discharging the patient from the emergency department without admission and full neurological workup
key_reasoning_points: The subacute progression from psychiatric symptoms to seizures, orolingual dyskinesias, and autonomic instability in a young woman is the hallmark phenotype of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Extreme delta brush on EEG is highly specific for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and is not characteristic of other encephalitides or psychiatric disorders
A preceding viral prodrome is reported in approximately 70% of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis cases and should heighten clinical suspicion
Hyponatremia secondary to SIADH is a common complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis due to hypothalamic dysfunction
Ovarian teratoma serves as the immunologic trigger in 40-50% of affected women; surgical resection is essential for treatment response and relapse prevention
CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies have higher sensitivity than serum antibodies; CSF testing must always be performed
HSV encephalitis must be empirically treated pending PCR results, as delayed treatment carries high mortality and morbidity

Family history of autoimmune disease (SLE in maternal aunt, rheumatoid arthritis in mother) suggests genetic predisposition to immune dysregulation, which may be relevant to the development of autoimmune encephalitis
